# Folosy Backend — API Reference

This document describes every HTTP endpoint the backend currently exposes. It
reflects the **implemented** behavior (verified end-to-end), not aspirational
design.

> Status as of Phase 5: Auth (register / login / refresh / Google) + `GET /me` +
> full Categories CRUD. Transactions, dashboard, and SMS endpoints are not built
> yet.

---

## Conventions

### Base URL
The server listens on `:8080`. All paths below are relative to that host, e.g.
`http://localhost:8080/auth/login`.

### Content type
- **Request bodies** are JSON. Send `Content-Type: application/json`.
- **Successful responses** are JSON (`Content-Type: application/json`).
- **Error responses** are currently **plain text** (`text/plain`), produced by
  `http.Error` — the body is a short human-readable message, not a JSON
  envelope. (A structured JSON error shape is a likely future refinement.)

### Authentication
Protected endpoints require a **bearer access token** in the header:

```
Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
```

- The access token is a short-lived (~15 min) **HS256 JWT** obtained from
  `/auth/login`, `/auth/refresh`, or `/auth/google`.
- It is **algorithm-pinned** on verify (HMAC only) — forged `alg:none` / RS256
  tokens are rejected.
- On any auth failure (missing header, wrong scheme, expired/tampered/forged
  token) a protected route returns **`401`** with body `unauthorized` and never
  reveals which check failed.
- The token TTLs are configurable via `JWT_ACCESS_TTL` / `JWT_REFRESH_TTL`
  (defaults ~15 min / ~7 days).

### Money format
All monetary values are **integer minor units (cents)** — never floats or
decimal strings. `budget_limit_minor: 2999` means **$29.99**. The client is
responsible for the decimal conversion at display (`÷100`) and input (`×100`,
reject >2 decimal places). A fractional value (e.g. `212.3`) is invalid and is
rejected at JSON decode (`400`) because the field is a Go `int64`.

### Timestamps
`created_at` / `updated_at` are RFC 3339 / ISO 8601 UTC strings, e.g.
`2026-06-27T03:07:44Z`.

### Status codes used
| Code | Meaning in this API |
|------|---------------------|
| `200 OK` | Successful read / update / login |
| `201 Created` | Resource created (`register`, create category) |
| `204 No Content` | Successful delete (no body) |
| `400 Bad Request` | Malformed JSON, body too large, or validation failure |
| `401 Unauthorized` | Bad credentials / token, or invalid refresh / Google token |
| `404 Not Found` | Resource doesn't exist **or isn't owned by the caller** |
| `409 Conflict` | Uniqueness violation (duplicate email / category name) |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Unexpected server-side failure (generic body) |

### Request body limits
Auth and category write bodies are capped (`MaxBytesReader`); an oversized body
fails fast with `400`.

---

## Data types

### Category
```json
{
  "id": "e5c14040-4024-48d8-ad58-0a55f5d6de45",
  "name": "Groceries",
  "icon": "cart",
  "budget_limit_minor": 50000,
  "created_at": "2026-06-27T03:07:44Z",
  "updated_at": "2026-06-27T03:07:44Z"
}
```
- `icon` — an opaque presentation label chosen and rendered by the client (an
  icon-name key or an emoji). `""` means "no icon". The server never interprets
  it.
- `budget_limit_minor` — budget in cents (see [Money format](#money-format)).
  `0` = no limit set.
- The owning `user_id` is **never** included in responses (it's always the
  caller).
- `spent` is **not** a field — spending is derived from transactions
  (forthcoming), not stored on the category.

---

## Auth endpoints (public)

### `POST /auth/register`
Create a new email/password account. Does **not** log the user in or return
tokens — the client follows up with `/auth/login`.

**Request**
```json
{ "email": "alice@example.com", "username": "alice", "password": "password123" }
```
Validation: `email` required, `username` required, `password` ≥ 8 characters.

**Response** — `201 Created`
```json
{ "email": "alice@example.com", "username": "alice" }
```

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `400` | Malformed/oversized body or validation failure |
| `409` | Email already registered (`email already exists`) |
| `500` | Server error |

---

### `POST /auth/login`
Exchange credentials for a token pair.

**Request**
```json
{ "email": "alice@example.com", "password": "password123" }
```

**Response** — `200 OK`
```json
{ "access_token": "<jwt>", "refresh_token": "<opaque-token>" }
```

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `400` | Malformed/oversized body or missing email/password |
| `401` | Wrong email or password — **and** Google-only accounts (no password). Generic `invalid email or password`, no account-existence leak |
| `500` | Server error |

---

### `POST /auth/refresh`
Rotate a refresh token for a **brand-new** access + refresh pair (single-use
rotation). The old refresh token is revoked.

**Request**
```json
{ "refresh_token": "<opaque-token>" }
```

**Response** — `200 OK`
```json
{ "access_token": "<jwt>", "refresh_token": "<new-opaque-token>" }
```

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `400` | Missing token |
| `401` | Unknown, expired, or already-used/revoked token (generic `invalid refresh token`). Presenting an already-revoked token also revokes **all** of that user's tokens (reuse/theft detection) |
| `500` | Server error |

> The client must **serialize** refresh calls — two concurrent `/auth/refresh`
> with the same token can trip the reuse detector and log the user out.

---

### `POST /auth/google`
Sign in with a Google **ID token** (obtained by the client from Google). The
backend verifies it (RS256, `aud` pinned to our client ID) and issues **our own**
token pair. First-time Google users are created; an existing account with the
same verified email is linked.

**Request**
```json
{ "id_token": "<google-id-token>" }
```

**Response** — `200 OK`
```json
{ "access_token": "<jwt>", "refresh_token": "<opaque-token>" }
```

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `400` | Missing `id_token` |
| `401` | Token failed verification, or missing `email` / unverified email (generic `invalid google token`) |
| `500` | Server error |

---

## Account endpoints (protected)

### `GET /me`
Return the authenticated user's own profile.

**Headers** — `Authorization: Bearer <access_token>`

**Response** — `200 OK`
```json
{ "id": "uuid", "email": "alice@example.com", "username": "alice" }
```

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `401` | Missing/invalid token, or the account no longer exists |
| `500` | Server error |

---

## Category endpoints (protected)

All category routes require `Authorization: Bearer <access_token>` and operate
**only** on categories owned by the caller. A category that doesn't exist and one
owned by another user are indistinguishable — both return `404`.

### `POST /categories`
Create a category.

**Request**
```json
{ "name": "Groceries", "icon": "cart", "budget_limit_minor": 50000 }
```
Validation: `name` required and ≤ 255 characters; `budget_limit_minor` ≥ 0.
`icon` is optional.

**Response** — `201 Created` → a [Category](#category) object.

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `400` | Malformed/oversized body or validation failure |
| `401` | Not authenticated |
| `409` | The caller already has a category with this name (`category name already exists`) |
| `500` | Server error |

---

### `GET /categories`
List all of the caller's categories, oldest first.

**Response** — `200 OK` → a JSON array of [Category](#category) objects.
Returns `[]` (not `null`) when the caller has none.

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `401` | Not authenticated |
| `500` | Server error |

---

### `GET /categories/{id}`
Fetch one of the caller's categories by id.

**Response** — `200 OK` → a [Category](#category) object.

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `401` | Not authenticated |
| `404` | No such category, or not owned by the caller |
| `500` | Server error |

---

### `PUT /categories/{id}`
Replace a category's mutable fields. This is a **full replace** — send the
complete desired state; an omitted `icon` is cleared to `""`.

**Request**
```json
{ "name": "Food & Drink", "icon": "fork", "budget_limit_minor": 65000 }
```
Validation: same as create.

**Response** — `200 OK` → the updated [Category](#category) object (with a bumped
`updated_at`).

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `400` | Malformed/oversized body or validation failure |
| `401` | Not authenticated |
| `404` | No such category, or not owned by the caller |
| `409` | Rename collides with another of the caller's categories |
| `500` | Server error |

---

### `DELETE /categories/{id}`
Delete one of the caller's categories.

**Response** — `204 No Content` (empty body).

**Errors**
| Code | When |
|------|------|
| `401` | Not authenticated |
| `404` | No such category, or not owned by the caller |
| `500` | Server error |

---

## Example flow

```bash
# 1. Register
curl -X POST localhost:8080/auth/register \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"alice@example.com","username":"alice","password":"password123"}'

# 2. Login -> capture access_token
curl -X POST localhost:8080/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"alice@example.com","password":"password123"}'

# 3. Create a category (use the access_token from step 2)
curl -X POST localhost:8080/categories \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <access_token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name":"Groceries","icon":"cart","budget_limit_minor":50000}'

# 4. List your categories
curl localhost:8080/categories -H 'Authorization: Bearer <access_token>'
```
